title: CERN, Large Hadron Collider (LHC), and Matter-Antimatter Asymmetry
focus: Institutional details of CERN, functioning of the LHC, and the fundamental physics concept of matter-antimatter asymmetry.
rationale: This section covers CERN's institutional facts, the technical aspects of the LHC, and the concept of matter-antimatter asymmetry, which are highly relevant for UPSC Science & Tech questions.
sectionExcerpt: Breakthrough Prize 2025 awarded to researchers at CERN's Large Hadron Collider (LHC) for discoveries including matter-antimatter asymmetry. CERN is an international scientific organization on the Franco-Swiss border; India is an Associate Member. Major achievements include confirming the Higgs boson (God particle) and developing the World Wide Web. The LHC is the world's largest particle accelerator using superconducting magnets. Matter-Antimatter Asymmetry refers to the imbalance between matter and antimatter in the universe, despite the Big Bang theoretically producing equal amounts. CERN observed CP violation in baryons, a major milestone in understanding this asymmetry.
evidenceBullets: India is an Associate Member of CERN, an international scientific organization located on the Franco-Swiss border.
CERN's LHC confirmed the existence of the Higgs boson (God particle) and developed the World Wide Web.
The LHC uses superconducting magnets to accelerate two high-energy particle beams close to the speed of light before collision.
Four experimental collaborations at CERN's LHC include ATLAS, CMS, ALICE, and LHCb.
Matter-Antimatter Asymmetry refers to the observable universe consisting almost entirely of matter, despite the Big Bang producing equal amounts of both.
CP symmetry implies physics laws remain unchanged if particles are swapped with antiparticles and viewed in a mirror.

title: Ordinary Matter, Dark Matter, and Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs)
focus: Detection of missing ordinary matter using FRBs, distribution of ordinary matter, comparison with dark matter, and the nature of FRBs.
rationale: This section contrasts ordinary and dark matter and introduces Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) as a cosmological tool, forming a distinct and highly testable conceptual cluster for UPSC Prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Researchers detected the universe's missing ordinary matter using Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs). Ordinary matter (baryonic matter) includes gas, dust, stars, and planets, interacting with electromagnetic radiation. It is distributed 76% in the intergalactic medium, 15% in galaxy halos, and 9% within galaxies. Dark matter makes up about 27% of the cosmos, remains invisible as it doesn't interact with light, but exerts gravitational force like ordinary matter. FRBs are blasts of electromagnetic radiation (radio waves) lasting milliseconds to seconds. They act as cosmic flashlights, dispersing into different wavelengths to reveal the amount of matter in their path.
evidenceBullets: Ordinary matter (baryonic matter) interacts with electromagnetic radiation and is composed of baryons like protons and neutrons.
76% of ordinary matter is distributed in the intergalactic medium (IGM), the thin gas between galaxies.
Dark matter makes up about 27% of the cosmos, does not interact with light, but exerts gravitational force and has mass.
Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) are blasts of electromagnetic radiation in the form of radio waves, lasting from milliseconds to seconds.
FRBs act as cosmic flashlights, dispersing into different wavelengths to measure matter distribution across the cosmic web.
